source.title: Post-Convergence Sim-to-Real Policy Transfer: A Principled Alternative to Cherry-Picking

confidence_rationale: Preprint (not peer-reviewed) but directly addresses the strategy-calibration dissociation problem in sim-to-real transfer. Uses Spearman rank correlation between simulated and real reward rankings as the primary metric -- direct simulated performance achieves SCC ~0.25 (weak prediction), worst-case-weighted estimate achieves SCC ~0.55 (improved). Formally proves that policy structural robustness operates independently from absolute reward magnitudes. Maps to MECH-195 (strategy transfer, magnitude re-anchoring at episode close) and MECH-196 (frame collapse triggers recalibration). Transfer risk is medium: sim-to-real gap in robotics is analogous to but not identical with play-to-real transition in REE. The key difference is that sim-to-real involves a simulator with wrong physics (calibration error), while REE play involves deliberate synthetic signals (frame tag).
failure_signatures: Preprint: not peer-reviewed. The worst-case optimization approach may overfit to specific dynamics distributions.

The paper's focus is on policy selection after training convergence, not on detecting calibration leakage during the transfer itself -- it does not provide a real-time frame-collapse detection metric.

Spearman rank correlation measures consistency of policy rankings, not the magnitude of calibration error -- a policy could rank correctly in sim but have severely miscalibrated reward thresholds in real.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Post-convergence sim-to-real policy transfer shows strong dissociation between policy ranking (strategy structure) and absolute reward magnitude. Worst-case performance optimization improves rank correlation (SCC 0.25 -> 0.55) without requiring the sim-real gap to be small. Policy structural robustness operates independently from absolute reward magnitudes (proven by Theorem 1).
mapping.ree_translation: This is the computational evidence closest to MECH-195's strategy-calibration dissociation for the DEV-NEED-011 constructive play gate. The SCC metric is directly applicable as a 'calibration leak detector': if strategy structure ranked in play (synthetic signals) correlates with strategy structure in real episodes, strategy has transferred. If the correlation is mediated by reward magnitude (high-reward-in-play = high-reward-in-real), calibration has leaked. The worst-case optimization framing is analogous to MECH-196's immediate recalibration on frame collapse: the agent should not assume synthetic magnitudes persist in real episodes.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The sim-to-real gap in robotics involves wrong physics (dynamics mismatch), while REE play involves deliberate synthetic reward signals. These are structurally different calibration problems. The Spearman rank correlation metric measures policy ranking consistency, not REE's specific concern of whether E3 trajectory structure transfers independently of z_goal/z_harm magnitude. Requires significant adaptation to be useful as a DEV-NEED-011 gate metric.
mapping.source_context: Computational RL and robotic control. Post-convergence policy selection problem. MuJoCo-style continuous control environments.

confidence_components.notes: Preprint reduces source quality. Mapping fidelity moderate: dissociation principle maps well but the specific mechanism (dynamics mismatch vs synthetic signals) is different. Transfer risk moderate.
